funFacts: A ring-tailed lemur's tail is longer than its whole body and has 13 black and 13 white rings.
On cool mornings, lemurs sit up and stretch their arms out to soak up the warm sunshine.
Male lemurs have 'stink fights,' rubbing smelly scent on their tails and waving them at rivals.
Ring-tailed lemurs live only on Madagascar and are found nowhere else in the wild.
Lemur troops are led by a top female, who gets first choice of food and the best resting spots.
They are one of the most ground-loving lemurs, spending lots of time walking on the forest floor.

faq.question: What is a ring-tailed lemur?
faq.answer: A ring-tailed lemur is a cat-sized primate from Madagascar. It is best known for its long, black-and-white ringed tail and for living in lively groups called troops.

faq.question: Where do ring-tailed lemurs live?
faq.answer: Ring-tailed lemurs live only on the island of Madagascar, mostly in the dry forests and spiny scrub of the south and southwest. They are found nowhere else in the wild.

faq.question: What do ring-tailed lemurs eat?
faq.answer: Ring-tailed lemurs are omnivores. They mostly eat fruit and leaves, especially from tamarind trees, plus flowers, sap, and sometimes insects.

faq.question: Why do lemurs sunbathe?
faq.answer: On cool mornings, ring-tailed lemurs sit up and spread their arms to warm their bellies in the sun. It helps them heat up before a busy day of feeding and traveling.

faq.question: Why is a lemur's tail striped?
faq.answer: The black-and-white rings make the tail easy to see. Lemurs hold their tails high like flags to help the troop stay together as they move through the forest.

faq.question: Are ring-tailed lemurs endangered?
faq.answer: Yes. Ring-tailed lemurs are listed as Endangered because they are losing their forest homes and are sometimes hunted or taken for the pet trade.
